description: Eval cases for Power BI report visualization design skill

test_cases.name: Executive dashboard chart selection
test_cases.input: I'm designing an executive dashboard showing: monthly revenue trend (12 months), top 5 product categories by revenue, current month vs target KPIs for 4 metrics, and regional breakdown. What charts should I use?
test_cases.evaluation_notes: Must recommend: line for 12-month trend, bar/column for top 5 categories, KPI cards for targets, consider map or bar for regions

test_cases.name: Accessibility improvement
test_cases.input: My Power BI report uses red and green colors to show performance vs target. Some users are colorblind. How should I fix this?
test_cases.evaluation_notes: Must recommend adding patterns/icons alongside color, not relying on red/green alone, check contrast ratios

test_cases.name: Too many visuals on page
test_cases.input: My report page has 20 visuals and feels cluttered. Users say it's overwhelming. How do I redesign it?
test_cases.evaluation_notes: Must recommend reducing to 6-8 visuals, using drill-through for detail, bookmarks or tabs for alternatives, information hierarchy

test_cases.name: Mobile layout design
test_cases.input: Our field sales team primarily uses Power BI on mobile phones. How should I design the report layout?
test_cases.evaluation_notes: Must address portrait orientation, touch targets (44px min), simplified navigation, card visuals, stacked layout

test_cases.name: Composition chart choice
test_cases.input: I want to show the breakdown of our revenue by 12 product categories as a percentage of total. What visualization is best?
test_cases.evaluation_notes: Must flag that pie/donut is not ideal for 12 categories (max 5-7), recommend treemap or stacked bar as alternatives
